title: Multi-Agent Systems in Practice
subtitle: One agent is useful. A team of agents is powerful. Build pipelines, coordinate handoffs, and manage agent teams.
heroTitle: Multi-Agent Systems in Practice

objectives.title: Build a Multi-Agent Pipeline
objectives.description: Go from Session 6's design exercise to a working system: build a Researcher agent that feeds into an Analyst agent, then add an Editor for quality control.

objectives.title: Master Agent Coordination Patterns
objectives.description: Implement handoffs, shared context, and orchestration. Learn when pipeline, debate, and orchestrator patterns are the right choice.

objectives.title: Handle Information Distortion
objectives.description: The 'telephone game' problem: information degrades as it passes between agents. Build safeguards against compounding errors.

objectives.title: Apply the Org Chart Analogy
objectives.description: Multi-agent systems mirror organizational design: division of labor, specialization, coordination costs. Map business structures to agent architectures.

agenda.title: Part 1: From Design to Build
agenda.description: In Session 6 you designed multi-agent systems on paper. Today you build one.
bulletPoints: Recap: Three architectures from Session 6 — Pipeline, Debate, Orchestrator
Today's build: A 2-agent pipeline (Researcher → Analyst), then extend to 3 agents (+ Editor)
The org chart analogy: Why companies have departments. Why agent systems need specialization.
Key question: When does adding another agent help vs. adding complexity?

agenda.title: Part 2: Agent Communication Patterns
agenda.description: How agents talk to each other — message passing, shared context, and orchestration.
bulletPoints: Message passing: Agent A's output becomes Agent B's input. The simplest coordination pattern.
Shared context: All agents read from and write to a shared workspace. More flexible but harder to debug.
Orchestration: A manager agent decides who works on what. The pattern behind Claude Code's team feature.
Live demo: See how a simple pipeline actually works in code — it's just function calls with structured outputs.

agenda.title: Part 3: Hands-On — Build a 2-Agent Pipeline
agenda.description: Build a Researcher → Analyst pipeline, then extend it with an Editor agent.
bulletPoints: Phase 1 (15 min): Build the Researcher agent — searches for information, returns structured findings
Phase 2 (15 min): Build the Analyst agent — receives research, identifies patterns, generates insights and recommendations
Phase 3 (15 min): Add the Editor agent — fact-checks, improves clarity, scores quality. Run the full pipeline end-to-end.

agenda.title: Break
agenda.description: You now have a team of AI agents that collaborate. Next: what can go wrong?

agenda.title: Part 5: The Telephone Game — Information Distortion
agenda.description: The ethics of multi-agent systems: who's accountable when information degrades across agents?
bulletPoints: Ethics moment: The 'telephone game' — each agent subtly changes information. After 3 agents, is the output still accurate?
Live experiment: Run the same question through your pipeline 3 times. Do you get the same answer? Why not?
Accountability: If Agent C gives a wrong answer based on Agent A's research, who's responsible?
Safeguards: Citation chains, confidence propagation, and human review gates at critical handoffs.
Business value: Division of labor works in companies AND in agent systems — but coordination has a cost.

agenda.title: Part 6: When Multi-Agent is Worth It
agenda.description: Multi-agent isn't always better. Learn the decision framework.
bulletPoints: The complexity tax: Each agent adds latency, cost, and potential failure points
Decision framework: Use multi-agent when tasks require different expertise, when quality review is critical, or when you need adversarial checking
Stick with single-agent when the task is straightforward, speed matters more than thoroughness, or the pipeline is linear and simple
Real examples: Content creation (multi-agent wins), simple Q&A (single-agent wins), code review (debate pattern wins)

agenda.title: Part 7: Wrap-Up + Homework
agenda.description: You've built single agents, knowledge agents, and now agent teams. Next: apply them to YOUR industry.
bulletPoints: The stack: Tools (S4) → Real APIs (S5) → ReAct (S6) → RAG (S7) → Multi-Agent (TODAY)
Next session: Agents for Your Industry — pick your department, build your agent
Homework preview: Extend your pipeline, analyze information distortion, design a domain-specific multi-agent system

resources.title: Multi-Agent Workshop Notebook
resources.description: Build a Researcher → Analyst → Editor pipeline. Learn agent communication, handoffs, and quality control in a multi-agent system.

resources.title: Advanced Agent Patterns
resources.description: Review the visual explainer from Session 6: Pipeline, Debate, and Orchestrator architectures. Today you build what you designed.

resources.title: Anthropic Multi-Agent Guide
resources.description: Official documentation on building multi-agent systems with Claude. Covers orchestration, handoffs, and coordination patterns.

resources.title: Building Effective Agents (Anthropic)
resources.description: Anthropic's guide on when to use multi-agent vs. single-agent approaches. Includes decision frameworks and real-world examples.

activities.title: Build a Multi-Agent Pipeline
activities.description: 45 minutes in Google Colab. Build a team of specialized agents:

steps.title: Build the Researcher Agent (15 min)
steps.description: An agent that searches for information on a topic and returns structured findings: key facts, sources, and confidence levels.

steps.title: Build the Analyst Agent (15 min)
steps.description: Receives the Researcher's output, identifies patterns, generates insights, and writes recommendations. Tests: Does it add value beyond summarizing?

steps.title: Add the Editor Agent (15 min)
steps.description: Fact-checks the Analyst's report, improves clarity, and scores overall quality 1-10. Run the full 3-agent pipeline end-to-end.

activities.title: Telephone Game Experiment
activities.description: Test information distortion across your agent pipeline:

steps.title: Run 3 Times (10 min)
steps.description: Send the same question through your pipeline 3 times. Compare the final outputs. How much do they differ?

steps.title: Trace the Distortion (10 min)
steps.description: Read each agent's intermediate output. Where did information change? Was it the Researcher, Analyst, or Editor?

steps.title: Add Safeguards (10 min)
steps.description: Implement citation chains: each agent must cite where it got each fact. Re-run and compare accuracy.

takeaways.text: Agent teams mirror org charts. Division of labor, specialization, and coordination costs apply to AI agents just like human teams. Design your agent system like you'd design a department.
Watch for the telephone game. Information degrades across agents. Citation chains and confidence propagation are your safeguards against compounding errors.
Multi-agent isn't always better. Each agent adds latency, cost, and complexity. Use the decision framework: multi-agent for complex, multi-expertise tasks. Single-agent for everything else.
The pipeline pattern is your workhorse. Research → Analysis → Review is the most common multi-agent pattern. Master it and you can adapt it to any domain.

homework.label: Extend:
homework.text: Add a 4th agent to your pipeline (e.g., a Fact-Checker, Summarizer, or Domain Expert). Test the full pipeline with 3 business questions.

homework.label: Analyze:
homework.text: Run your pipeline 5 times on the same question. Document how outputs differ, where distortion occurs, and what safeguards would help.

homework.label: Design:
homework.text: Propose a multi-agent system for your target industry (the one you'll use for your final project). Include: agent roles, handoff logic, human gates, and cost estimate.

homework.label: Read:
homework.text: Read about Claude Code's multi-agent 'team' feature. Write a paragraph on how it applies the orchestrator pattern from Session 6.

nextSession.title: Session 9: Agents for Your Industry
nextSession.description: Pick your department. Build your agent. Marketing, finance, HR, or operations — apply everything you've learned to YOUR career.
